findings.title: Dual temp_views / temp_views_lower variables create a latent case-sensitivity trap across the function
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the original `temp_views` parameter name inside the function entirely. Accept `temp_views: set[str]` at the signature, immediately rebind it to its lowercased version (`temp_views = {v.lower() for v in temp_views}`), and delete `temp_views_lower`. Every subsequent reference in the function body (lines 723, 739, 764, 771, 789, 792) already uses `temp_views_lower` consistently — the rename becomes a pure drop-in. This removes the parallel-variable pattern and makes it impossible for a future edit to reach for the wrong one. The public signature is unchanged because callers pass arbitrary-case sets and the lowercasing happens once at entry.

findings.title: The original `temp_views` parameter name is unused after line 717 yet remains in scope, creating silent ambiguity
findings.suggested_fix: After introducing `temp_views_lower`, the parameter `temp_views` is referenced only at line 711 (`if not temp_views:`) and line 717 (the lowering step). It is never used again, but because it shares a very similar name with `temp_views_lower`, any reader or future author must actively verify both variables are not in simultaneous use. The fast-path guard at line 711 should use `temp_views` (checking the raw input is fine there), but document this explicitly with a comment such as `# use raw set for the fast-path; all further access is via temp_views_lower`.

findings.title: max_iterations heuristic silently under-counts when the input set has duplicates before lowercasing
findings.suggested_fix: Line 764 computes `max_iterations = len(temp_views_lower) + 1`. If the caller passes a mixed-case set where two entries lower-case to the same name (e.g. `{'MY_VIEW', 'my_view'}`), `temp_views_lower` collapses them and `max_iterations` would be correct, but this is a subtle implicit deduplication contract that is not documented. Add a one-line assertion or comment: `# temp_views_lower deduplicates; len() gives the true number of distinct views`.

findings.title: Test docstring references the internal variable name (`temp_views_lower`) as an implementation detail
findings.suggested_fix: The docstring at line 841-842 reads '...the lookup was using original-case temp_views set instead of temp_views_lower...'. Tests should be coupled to observable behaviour, not internal variable names. Rewrite the docstring to describe the observable failure: 'uppercase temp view names defined in one cell were not resolved when referenced in a later cell, because the membership check was case-sensitive'.

residual_risks: The fix is narrowly correct (one membership check was using the wrong variable), but the dual-variable pattern across ~120 lines of the same function means a future patch could introduce the same class of bug at any of the five other `temp_views_lower` membership checks. Consolidating to a single normalized variable (see finding 1) is the durable fix.

No test covers a chain of temp views where an intermediate view is defined with mixed case AND the final consumer references it by different case than the intermediate was lowercased to. The existing new test covers a single-hop scenario only.
testing_gaps: No test for a two-hop temp-view chain (A -> INTERMEDIATE_VIEW -> B) where INTERMEDIATE_VIEW is defined with uppercase and referenced with lowercase in the second hop.
No test verifies that the wildcard-source branch (`src.endswith('.*')`) inside `_lookup` also behaves correctly when the temp view name was uppercase in the original `temp_views` set.
